info.text: CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences, 26 November 2020, Shift 2. Part A: General Aptitude (Q1-20). Marks: +2, -0.5. Attempt any 15.

name.text: NOV2020 Q1 - Find f(0)
NOV2020 Q2 - Shortest distance between parallel lines

questiontext.text: The shortest distance between parallel lines A (\(y=x\)) and B (\(y=x-2\)) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(\sqrt{2}\)

Distance \(=\frac{|0-(-2)|}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q3 - Minimum and maximum cost

questiontext.text: Two varieties A, B of rice cost Rs.30 and Rs.90/kg; varieties C, D of pulses cost Rs.100 and Rs.120/kg. Constraints: at least 1 kg each of A, B and at least 0.5 kg each of C, D; total 5 kg. Minimum and maximum costs are respectively

name.text: NOV2020 Q4 - Logic puzzle: crime and twins

questiontext.text: One of A, B, C, D committed a crime. A and D always tell the truth; B and C always lie. C and D are identical twins. A says "D is innocent"; B says "A is guilty"; among C and D, one says "A is innocent" and the other says "B is guilty". Which of the following is FALSE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) C is innocent

A (truthful): D is innocent. B (liar): "A is guilty" is a lie, so A is innocent. D (truthful twin) says "B is guilty" ⇒ B is guilty. Hence C committed the crime, so "C is innocent" is FALSE.

name.text: NOV2020 Q5 - Venn diagram: quadruped, mammal, whale, lizard

questiontext.text: Which Venn diagram represents the relations between: quadruped, mammal, whale, house lizard? (Whale is a mammal but not a quadruped; house lizard is a quadruped but not a mammal.)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Diagram 4

Mammals and quadrupeds partially overlap. Whale lies in the mammal-only region; house lizard in the quadruped-only region. Diagram 4 correctly shows this partial overlap configuration.

name.text: NOV2020 Q6 - Height of water poured from tube into bucket

questiontext.text: A 7 m long tube with inner diameter 2 cm is filled with water and poured into a cylindrical bucket of inner base area 200 cm\(^2\). Approximate height (cm) of water in the bucket is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 11

Volume of water: \(\pi r^2 L = \pi(1)^2(700) = 700\pi \approx 2199\) cm\(^3\). Height: \(700\pi/200 = 3.5\pi \approx 11\) cm.

name.text: NOV2020 Q7 - Rate of height increase in cone

questiontext.text: Water fills a cone from the top at a constant volumetric rate. The rate of increase of the height of the water column

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) increases as cube-root of the volumetric rate

For cone with half-angle \(\alpha\): \(V=\frac{1}{3}\pi\tan^2\!\alpha\cdot h^3\), so \(h\propto V^{1/3}\). With constant volumetric rate \(Q\), \(V=Qt\), so \(h\propto t^{1/3}\) and \(dh/dt\propto t^{-2/3}\), which decreases over time.

answer.text: depends on the apex angle of the cone
increases as cube-root of the volumetric rate
increases as square-root of the volumetric rate

name.text: NOV2020 Q8 - Probability at least 2 bullets in same square

questiontext.text: A square board is divided into 9 identical small squares. Three bullets are shot randomly (each with equal probability of hitting any square). \(P(\text{at least 2 bullets hit the same small square}) =\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 25/81

P(all 3 in different squares) \(= 1\cdot\frac{8}{9}\cdot\frac{7}{9}=\frac{56}{81}\). So P(at least 2 in same) \(=1-\frac{56}{81}=\frac{25}{81}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q9 - Absorbance ratio of mixture

questiontext.text: Wavelength-dependent absorbance of compounds A and B is shown. Let \(R=\) absorbance at 650 nm / absorbance at 950 nm. If the mixture contains 95% compound A, then \(R\) must be

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 1

With 95% compound A dominating, \(R\approx R_A\). From the figure, compound A has equal absorbance at both wavelengths, so \(R=1\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q10 - Epidemic spread rate vs infected count

questiontext.text: An epidemic spreads in a population of size \(P\). The rate \(R\propto N(P-N)\) where \(N\) = number infected. The correct graph of \(R\) vs \(N\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Symmetric bell-shape peaking at \(N=P/2\)

\(R=kN(P-N)\) is a downward-opening parabola with zeros at \(N=0\) and \(N=P\), maximum at \(N=P/2\): a symmetric bell shape.

name.text: NOV2020 Q11 - Work efficiency of A, B, C

questiontext.text: A and B together complete a work in 30 days; B and C in 24 days; C and A in 28 days. Which conclusion is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) C most efficient and A least efficient

Let \(a,b,c\) be daily rates. \(a+b=1/30,\ b+c=1/24,\ c+a=1/28\). Solving with LCM(30,24,28)=840: \(a=23/1680,\ b=33/1680,\ c=37/1680\). Since \(c>b>a\): C most efficient, A least efficient.

answer.text: C most efficient, B least efficient
B most efficient; least cannot be determined
C most efficient; least cannot be determined
C most efficient and A least efficient

name.text: NOV2020 Q12 - Clock A time when clock C shows Wednesday 2 pm

questiontext.text: Clock A loses 4 min/hr; B is correct; C gains 3 min/hr. On Monday, all show 8 pm. On the following Wednesday, when C shows 2 pm, clock A shows

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 9:20 am

C advances at 63 min/real hr. C shows Wed 2 pm, which is 42 h after Mon 8 pm. Real elapsed time: \(T=42\times(60/63)=40\) h. A advances 56 min/real hr, reads \(40\times(56/60)=37\frac{1}{3}\) h after Mon 8 pm = Wed 9:20 am.

name.text: NOV2020 Q13 - Number of students from stationery count

questiontext.text: In a class: 1 pencil per 2 students; 1 eraser per 3 students; 1 ruler per 4 students. Total stationery items \(=65\). Number of students is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 60

\(\frac{n}{2}+\frac{n}{3}+\frac{n}{4}=65 \Rightarrow \frac{13n}{12}=65 \Rightarrow n=60\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q14 - Highest density water sample

questiontext.text: Four water samples: A (salinity 36, 12°C); B (salinity 36, 16°C); C (salinity 32, 12°C); D (salinity 32, 16°C). Which has the highest density?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) A

Higher salinity increases density; lower temperature increases density. Sample A has the highest salinity (36) and lowest temperature (12°C), giving the highest density.

name.text: NOV2020 Q15 - Day when recovered equals twice active cases

questiontext.text: COVID-19 data: Day 0 (active 990, recovered 760), Day 1 (1000, 800), Day 4 (1030, 920), Day 7 (1060, 1040), Day 10 (1090, 1160). Assuming linear trend, on which day will recovered cases equal twice the active cases?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 64 (NTA official key)

Active: \(A(n)=990+10n\); Recovered: \(R(n)=760+40n\). Set \(R=2A\): \(760+40n=1980+20n \Rightarrow n=61\). NTA official answer: Day 64.

name.text: NOV2020 Q16 - Time for A and B to complete course together

questiontext.text: Boat weighs 60 kg; oarsman A (80 kg); oarsman B (90 kg). At constant power, time \(\propto\) total weight. A alone: 1 hr; B alone: 1.5 hrs. Assuming powers add, time to complete together is

name.text: NOV2020 Q17 - Area of triangle OAE in parallelogram

questiontext.text: Parallelogram ABCD with centre O and E = midpoint of CD; base \(a\), height \(h\). Area of \(\triangle OAE\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\tfrac{1}{8}ah\)

Place A=(0,0), B=(a,0), C=(a,h), D=(0,h). O=(a/2,h/2), E=(a/2,h). Area of \(\triangle OAE=\frac{1}{2}|det\begin{pmatrix}a/2&h/2\\a/2&h\end{pmatrix}|=\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{ah}{4}=\frac{ah}{8}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q18 - Divisibility of sum of first n even numbers

questiontext.text: The sum of the first \(n\) even numbers is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Divisible by both \(n\) and \((n+1)\)

Sum \(=2+4+\cdots+2n=n(n+1)\), which is divisible by both \(n\) and \(n+1\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q19 - Angle in regular pentagram

questiontext.text: A, B, C, D, E are vertices of a regular pentagram (star figure). The angle \(\angle ABC\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) \(36^\circ\)

In a regular pentagram, each star-point is an isoceles triangle with apex angle \(36^\circ\) and base angles \(72^\circ\) each (since \(36+72+72=180^\circ\)).

name.text: NOV2020 Q20 - Poles to remove for 40 m spacing

questiontext.text: On a 200 m road, poles are placed at 20 m intervals (11 poles including endpoints). How many poles must be removed so that poles are at maximum 40 m intervals?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 5

Poles at 20 m spacing: 0, 20, 40, ..., 200 m (11 poles). For 40 m spacing: keep 0, 40, 80, 120, 160, 200 m (6 poles). Poles removed: \(11-6=5\).

info.text: CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences, 26 November 2020, Shift 2. Part B: Core Mathematics (Q21-60). Marks: +3, -0.75. Attempt any 25. Q49 and Q58 were officially DROPPED by NTA.

name.text: NOV2020 Q21 - liminf of sequence of sets

questiontext.text: Let \(\{E_n\}\) be a sequence of subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\). Define \(\limsup_n E_n=\bigcap_{k=1}^\infty\bigcup_{n=k}^\infty E_n\) and \(\liminf_n E_n=\bigcup_{k=1}^\infty\bigcap_{n=k}^\infty E_n\). Which statement is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\liminf E_n=\{x: x\in E_n \text{ for all but finitely many }n\}\)

\(\liminf E_n = \bigcup_k\bigcap_{n\geq k}E_n\) = set of \(x\) belonging to \(E_n\) for all sufficiently large \(n\), i.e., all but finitely many \(n\). The \(\limsup\) is the set belonging to infinitely many \(E_n\).

answer.text: \(\liminf E_n = \{x: x\in E_n\text{ for all but finitely many }n\}\)
\(\liminf E_n = \{x: x\in E_n\text{ for infinitely many }n\}\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q22 - NOT true for bounded f:N to N

questiontext.text: Let \(f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be a bounded function. Which statement is NOT true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}(f(n)+n)\in\mathbb{N}\)

Since \(f\) is bounded, \(f(n)\leq M\) for some \(M\in\mathbb{N}\). Then \(f(n)+n\to\infty\), so \(\liminf(f(n)+n)=+\infty\notin\mathbb{N}\). Statements (1) and (2) are true since \(f\) is bounded in \(\mathbb{N}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q23 - Continuous maps from R^2 to R

questiontext.text: Which statement about continuous maps from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) to \(\mathbb{R}\) is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) No continuous bijective maps from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) to \(\mathbb{R}\)

A bijective continuous map \(f:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\) would contradict invariance of domain / topological dimension theory. Removing a point disconnects \(\mathbb{R}\) but not \(\mathbb{R}^2\), so no bijection can be continuous.

answer.text: There are countably many continuous maps \(\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\)
There are finitely many continuous surjective maps \(\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\)
There are infinitely many continuous injective maps \(\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\)
No continuous bijective map from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) to \(\mathbb{R}\) exists

name.text: NOV2020 Q24 - Uniform convergence of alternating series

questiontext.text: The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n\sin nx}{n^{\log n}}\), \(x\in\mathbb{R}\), converges

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Uniformly for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\)

Since \(|\sin nx|\leq 1\), we have \(\left|\frac{(-1)^n\sin nx}{n^{\log n}}\right|\leq\frac{1}{n^{\log n}}\). Since \(\sum 1/n^{\log n}\) converges (by comparison with \(\sum 1/n^2\) for large \(n\)), the series converges uniformly by Weierstrass M-test.

name.text: NOV2020 Q25 - Subsequence with convergent series

questiontext.text: Given a sequence \((a_n)_{n\geq 1}\) of real numbers. Which statement is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) There exist \(b\) and a subsequence \((a_{n_k})\) such that \(\displaystyle\sum_{k\geq 1}\left|b-\frac{a_{n_k}}{1+|a_{n_k}|}\right|\) converges

The sequence \(c_n=\frac{a_n}{1+|a_n|}\in(-1,1)\) is bounded, so it has a convergent subsequence \(c_{n_k}\to b\). The tail of \(\sum|b-c_{n_k}|\) converges to 0, making the series convergent.

answer.text: There exists a subsequence \((a_{n_k})\) such that \(\displaystyle\sum_{k\geq 1}\frac{a_{n_k}}{1+|a_{n_k}|}\) converges
There exist \(b\) and a subsequence \((a_{n_k})\) such that \(\displaystyle\sum_{k\geq 1}\left|b-\frac{a_{n_k}}{1+|a_{n_k}|}\right|\) converges

name.text: NOV2020 Q26 - Common points of crossing continuous functions

questiontext.text: Let \(f, g\) be continuous on \([0,1]\) with \(f(0)=f(1)=0\), \(g(0)=g(1)=1\), and \(f(1/2)>g(1/2)\). Which is true about \(t\in[0,1]\) with \(f(t)=g(t)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) At least two such \(t\)

Let \(h=f-g\). Then \(h(0)=-1<0\), \(h(1/2)>0\), and \(h(1)=-1<0\). By IVT, \(h\) has a zero in \((0,1/2)\) and another in \((1/2,1)\). So there are at least two values of \(t\) with \(f(t)=g(t)\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q27 - Rank and distinct nonzero eigenvalues

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be an \(n\times n\) matrix with exactly \(r\) distinct nonzero eigenvalues. Which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\geq r\)

Each distinct nonzero eigenvalue contributes at least one linearly independent eigenvector to the column space. Hence \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\geq r\). The other options can fail: e.g., \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\) can exceed \(r\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q28 - Determinant identity for 2x2 matrices

questiontext.text: Let \(A, B\) be \(2\times 2\) matrices. Which identity is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(\det(A+B)+\det(A-B)=2\det A - 2\det B\)

For \(2\times 2\): write \(A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\), \(B=\begin{pmatrix}p&q\\r&s\end{pmatrix}\). Compute \(\det(A+B)+\det(A-B)\) directly and simplify: \(=2(ad-bc)-2(ps-qr)=2\det A-2\det B\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q29 - Compute A^20 for given 2x2 matrix

questiontext.text: If \(A=\begin{pmatrix}3&-2\\2&-1\end{pmatrix}\), then \(A^{20}\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(\begin{pmatrix}41&-40\\40&-39\end{pmatrix}\)

The eigenvalues of \(A\) are both 1 (repeated). The Jordan form is \(J=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\). Then \(A^n=\begin{pmatrix}1+2n&-2n\\2n&1-2n\end{pmatrix}\). For \(n=20\): \(A^{20}=\begin{pmatrix}41&-40\\40&-39\end{pmatrix}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q30 - Trace of A squared

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be a \(2\times 2\) real matrix with \(\det A=1\) and \(\mathrm{tr}(A)=3\). Then \(\mathrm{tr}(A^2)=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 7

If eigenvalues are \(\lambda_1,\lambda_2\), then \(\lambda_1+\lambda_2=3\) and \(\lambda_1\lambda_2=1\). So \(\mathrm{tr}(A^2)=\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)^2-2\lambda_1\lambda_2=9-2=7\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q31 - When does bilinear form define an inner product

questiontext.text: For \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\), let \(p(x,y)=a^2x_1y_1+abx_2y_1+abx_1y_2+b^2x_2y_2\). For what values of \(a,b\) does \(p\) define an inner product on \(\mathbb{R}^2\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) For no values of \(a, b\)

The matrix of \(p\) is \(\begin{pmatrix}a^2&ab\\ab&b^2\end{pmatrix}\), which has determinant \(a^2b^2-(ab)^2=0\). Since the matrix is always singular, \(p\) is never positive definite, hence never an inner product.

name.text: NOV2020 Q32 - Positive definite quadratic form

questiontext.text: Which real quadratic form on \(\mathbb{R}^2\) is positive definite?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(Q(X,Y)=X^2-XY+Y^2\)

Matrix is \(\begin{pmatrix}1&-1/2\\-1/2&1\end{pmatrix}\). Eigenvalues: \(\det=3/4>0\), trace\(=2>0\), so both eigenvalues positive. Positive definite. Option (3): \(X^2+2XY+Y^2=(X+Y)^2\geq 0\) but not positive definite.

name.text: NOV2020 Q33 - Contour integral over circle |z-1|=1

questiontext.text: Let \(\gamma\) be the positively oriented circle \(|z-1|=1\). Then \(\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\displaystyle\int_\gamma\frac{dz}{z^3-1}\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 1/3

\(z^3-1=(z-1)(z^2+z+1)\). The circle \(|z-1|=1\) encloses only the pole \(z=1\). Residue at \(z=1\): \(\lim_{z\to 1}\frac{z-1}{z^3-1}=\frac{1}{3z^2}\big|_{z=1}=1/3\). By residue theorem: result \(=1/3\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q34 - Holomorphic antiderivative of sin(z)/z^p

questiontext.text: For positive integer \(p\), consider \(f(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z^p}\), \(z\in\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\). For which values of \(p\) does there exist a holomorphic \(g:\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\to\mathbb{C}\) with \(f(z)=g'(z)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) All odd integers

\(\sin z/z^p\) has an antiderivative on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\) iff its residue at \(z=0\) is zero. The Laurent expansion of \(\sin z = z - z^3/6 + \cdots\), so \(\sin z/z^p\) has residue (coefficient of \(1/z\)) equal to zero when \(p\) is odd (since \(\sin z\) has only odd powers).

name.text: NOV2020 Q35 - Contour integral with essential singularity

questiontext.text: Let \(\gamma\) be the positively oriented circle \(|z-1|=\tfrac{1}{2}\). Then \(\displaystyle\int_\gamma\frac{ze^{1/z}}{z^2-1}\,dz\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(i\pi e\)

The integrand has poles at \(z=\pm 1\) and an essential singularity at \(z=0\). The circle \(|z-1|=1/2\) encloses only \(z=1\). Residue at \(z=1\): \(\lim_{z\to 1}\frac{z e^{1/z}}{z+1}=\frac{e}{2}\). Integral \(=2\pi i\cdot\frac{e}{2}=i\pi e\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q36 - Argument principle with power weight

questiontext.text: Let \(p\) be a positive integer, \(r(t)=e^{it}\), \(0\leq t<2\pi\). Let \(f\) be holomorphic in \(\{|z|<R\}\), \(R>1\), with unique zero at \(z_0\) (\(0<|z_0|<R\)) of multiplicity \(q\). Then \(\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\displaystyle\int_r\frac{f'(z)}{f(z)}z^p\,dz\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(qz_0^p\)

By the generalised argument principle (with weight \(z^p\)): \(\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_r\frac{f'}{f}z^p\,dz = \sum_{\text{zeros}}m_j z_j^p = q\cdot z_0^p\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q37 - Number theory: odd n divides (n-1)!

questiontext.text: Which statement is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Every odd \(n\geq 16\) divides \((n-1)!\)

If \(n\) is odd and composite with \(n\geq 9\), then \(n\mid(n-1)!\) (since all prime factors of \(n\) appear in \(\{1,\ldots,n-1\}\)). For odd prime \(p\), \(p\nmid(p-1)!\) by Wilson's theorem, but any odd composite \(n\geq 9\) satisfies \(n\mid(n-1)!\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q38 - Power set group operations

questiontext.text: Let \(X\) be non-empty with power set \(\mathcal{P}(X)\). Define \(A\star B=A\cap B\) and \(A\,\Delta\, B=(A\cup B)\setminus(A\cap B)\) (symmetric difference). Which is true about \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) under these operations?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) is a group under \(\Delta\), not under \(\star\)

Under \(\Delta\): identity is \(\emptyset\), inverse of \(A\) is \(A\) itself. This is a group (actually an abelian group). Under \(\star=\cap\): has identity \(X\) but no inverses (e.g., \(\emptyset\cap A=\emptyset\neq X\) for \(A\neq X\)). Not a group.

answer.text: \(\mathcal{P}(X)\) is a group under both \(\star\) and \(\Delta\)
\(\mathcal{P}(X)\) is a group under neither \(\star\) nor \(\Delta\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q39 - NOT true about Euler totient function

questiontext.text: Let \(\varphi(n)=|\{a:1\leq a\leq n,\gcd(a,n)=1\}|\) (Euler's totient). Which statement is NOT true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) There exists \(N>2\) such that for all \(n>N\), \(\varphi(N)<\varphi(n)\)

This is false: \(\varphi\) is not eventually increasing. For any \(N\), one can find \(n>N\) that is a product of small primes, making \(\varphi(n)/n\) small. The other statements (1),(2),(4) are true.

answer.text: There exists \(N>2\) such that for all \(n>N\), \(\varphi(N)<\varphi(n)\)
\(\{\varphi(n)/n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}\) has finitely many limit points

name.text: NOV2020 Q40 - Projection map is closed

questiontext.text: For metric spaces \((X,d_X),(Y,d_Y)\), call \(f:X\to Y\) closed if \(F\) closed \(\Rightarrow f(F)\) closed. With product metric \(d((x,y),(x',y'))=\max\{d_X(x,x'),d_Y(y,y')\}\), which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Projection \(p_1:X\times Y\to X\), \(p_1(x,y)=x\), is closed

With the max metric, a closed set in \(X\times Y\) projects to a closed set in \(X\). (Note: with product topology this would NOT generally be closed, but with the max metric the projection of closed sets is closed here.)

answer.text: For any \(A\subseteq X\), inclusion \(i:A\to X\) is closed
If \(g\circ f\) is closed, then \(g|_{f(X)}\) is closed
If \(f\) takes closed balls to closed sets, then \(f\) is closed

name.text: NOV2020 Q41 - Number of solutions of IVP

questiontext.text: Let \(k\) be a positive integer. For the IVP \(\dot{y}=y^{5k/(5k+2)}\), \(y(0)=0\), \(t>0\): how many \(C^1(0,\infty)\) solutions exist?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) At most two \(C^1(0,\infty)\) solutions

The exponent \(\alpha=5k/(5k+2)<1\), so uniqueness fails (Peano theorem gives existence). The two solutions are \(y\equiv 0\) and the non-trivial solution obtained by separating variables: \(y(t)=\left(\frac{2t}{5k+2}\right)^{(5k+2)/2}\). Hence exactly two solutions.

name.text: NOV2020 Q42 - Global solutions and finite-time blow-up

questiontext.text: Let \(y_0,z_0>0\), \(\alpha>1\). Consider (*) \(\dot{y}=y^\alpha\), \(y(0)=y_0\); and (**) \(\dot{z}=-z^\alpha\), \(z(0)=z_0\), \(t>0\). Which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) (**) has a global solution; \(\exists T<\infty\) s.t. \(\lim_{t\to T}|y(t)|=+\infty\)

For (*): \(y'=y^\alpha\) (\(\alpha>1\)) blows up in finite time \(T=\frac{1}{(\alpha-1)y_0^{\alpha-1}}\). For (**): \(z'=-z^\alpha\) gives \(z(t)\to 0\) as \(t\to\infty\) (global solution). Option (4) is correct.

answer.text: Neither (*) nor (**) has a global solution
(*) has a global solution; \(\exists T<\infty\) s.t. \(\lim_{t\to T}|z(t)|=+\infty\)
(**) has a global solution; \(\exists T<\infty\) s.t. \(\lim_{t\to T}|y(t)|=+\infty\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q43 - Surfaces perpendicular to family z^2=kxy

questiontext.text: The general solution of the surfaces perpendicular to the family \(z^2=kxy\), \(k\in\mathbb{R}\), is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\phi(x^2-y^2,\, 2x^2+z^2)=0\)

The orthogonal trajectories satisfy the PDE derived from the Lagrange-Charpit equations of the given family. Eliminating \(k\): normals to \(z^2=kxy\) give auxiliary equations \(dx/(2x)=dy/(-2y)=dz/(z)\), leading to invariants \(x^2-y^2\) and \(2x^2+z^2\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q44 - General solution of first-order PDE

questiontext.text: The general solution of \(x\,\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x}+y\,\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial y}=0\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(z=\phi(|x|/|y|)\), \(\phi\in C^1(\mathbb{R})\)

Lagrange auxiliary equations: \(dx/x=dy/y\), giving \(x/y=c\) as the first integral. The general solution is \(z=\phi(x/y)\), equivalently \(z=\phi(|x|/|y|)\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q45 - Lagrange and Hermite interpolation as epsilon to zero

questiontext.text: Let \(f\) be infinitely differentiable on a bounded interval \(I\). Take \(n\) interpolation points \(\{x_0,\ldots,x_{n-1}\}\) and additional points \(x_{n+j}=x_j+\varepsilon\) (\(j=0,\ldots,n-1\), all distinct). Let \(p_{2n-1}\) be the Lagrange interpolant at all \(2n\) points, and \(q_{2n-1}\) be the Hermite interpolant at \(\{x_0,\ldots,x_{n-1}\}\). As \(\varepsilon\to 0\), \(\sup_{x\in I}|p_{2n-1}(x)-q_{2n-1}(x)|\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Converges to 0

As \(\varepsilon\to 0\), the Lagrange interpolant at \(\{x_j, x_j+\varepsilon\}\) converges to the Hermite interpolant (which matches function and derivative values). Hence the difference converges to zero uniformly.

name.text: NOV2020 Q46 - Extremal of variational problem

questiontext.text: The extremal of \(J(y)=\displaystyle\int_0^1[2(y')^2+xy]\,dx\), \(y(0)=0\), \(y(1)=1\), \(y\in C^2[0,1]\), is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) \(y=\dfrac{x^3}{24}+\dfrac{23x}{24}\)

Euler-Lagrange equation: \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}-\frac{d}{dx}\frac{\partial L}{\partial y'}=0\Rightarrow x-4y''=0\Rightarrow y''=x/4\). Integrate twice: \(y=x^3/24+cx+d\). Apply \(y(0)=0\Rightarrow d=0\), \(y(1)=1\Rightarrow c=23/24\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q47 - Solution of Fredholm integral equation

questiontext.text: The solution of the Fredholm integral equation \(y(s)=s+2\displaystyle\int_0^1(st^2+s^2t)\,y(t)\,dt\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(y(s)=-(30s+40s^2)\)

Assume \(y(s)=As+Bs^2\). Substituting and matching coefficients using \(\int_0^1 t(At+Bt^2)\,dt = A/3+B/4\) and \(\int_0^1 t^2(At+Bt^2)\,dt = A/4+B/5\), solve the linear system to get \(A=-30\), \(B=-40\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q48 - Principal axis of solid hemisphere

questiontext.text: Consider the solid hemisphere \(S=\{(x,y,z):x^2+y^2+z^2\leq 1,\,z\geq 0\}\) of uniform density. Which statement is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) The \(z\)-axis is a principal axis for \(S\)

By symmetry about the \(z\)-axis, the inertia tensor is diagonal in the basis \(\{x,y,z\}\), making the \(z\)-axis a principal axis. The centre of mass is at \((0,0,3/8)\), not the origin.

answer.text: Centre of mass of \(S\) is at the origin
The \(x\)-axis is a principal axis for \(S\)
The moment of inertia tensor of \(S\) is not a diagonal matrix
The \(z\)-axis is a principal axis for \(S\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q49 - Bayesian probability (DROPPED by NTA)

questiontext.text: [DROPPED by NTA] In an MCQ exam, a student works out 50% of questions and guesses the rest (\(P(\text{correct}|\text{guessed})=0.30\)). When worked out, \(P(\text{wrong})=0.10\). Find \(P(\text{student guessed}\,|\,\text{answer is correct})\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 0.25 (Calculated; this question was DROPPED by NTA)

By Bayes: \(P(G|C)=\frac{P(C|G)P(G)}{P(C)}=\frac{0.30\times 0.5}{0.30\times 0.5+0.90\times 0.5}=\frac{0.15}{0.60}=0.25\). NTA dropped this question; all candidates received full marks.

name.text: NOV2020 Q50 - Limiting distribution of standardised chi-squared sum

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) be i.i.d. \(\chi^2(5)\). Let \(a\in\mathbb{R}\). The limiting distribution of \(a\!\left(\dfrac{X_1+\cdots+X_n-5n}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Standard normal for appropriate \(a\)

By CLT, \(\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_n-5n}{\sqrt{n}}\xrightarrow{d} N(0,\mathrm{Var}(X_1))=N(0,10)\). Choose \(a=1/\sqrt{10}\) to get \(N(0,1)\). So for appropriate \(a\), the limit is standard normal.

name.text: NOV2020 Q51 - NOT correct about communicating Markov chain states

questiontext.text: Consider a Markov chain with state space \(S\). Suppose \(i,j\in S\) communicate. Which is NOT correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) \(\lim_{n\to\infty}P(X_n=j|X_0=i)=\lim_{n\to\infty}P(X_n=j|X_0=j)\)

This is not generally true. The limit \(\lim_n P(X_n=j|X_0=i)\) depends on the initial state distribution and may differ from \(\lim_n P(X_n=j|X_0=j)\). Options (1)-(3) are correct properties of communicating states.

name.text: NOV2020 Q52 - Expected value of floor function of Uniform random variable

questiontext.text: Suppose \(X\sim\mathrm{Uniform}[0,100]\). Let \(Y=\lfloor X\rfloor\) (greatest integer \(\leq X\)). Which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(E(Y)=50\) (NTA key)

\(P(Y=k)=1/100\) for \(k=0,1,\ldots,99\). So \(E(Y)=\frac{1}{100}\sum_{k=0}^{99}k=\frac{99\times 50}{100}=49.5\). Also \(P(Y\leq 25)=26/100\) (not 1/4). NTA official answer: option (3) \(E(Y)=50\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q53 - Complete sufficient statistic for exponential family

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. with pdf \(f(x|\theta)=\dfrac{(\log\theta)\,\theta^x}{\theta-1}\), \(0<x<1\), \(\theta>1\). The statistic \(T=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Complete sufficient

The pdf belongs to a one-parameter exponential family with natural parameter \(\log\theta\) and sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\). By the completeness theorem for exponential families, \(T=\sum X_i\) is complete sufficient.

name.text: NOV2020 Q54 - UMP test for location parameter

questiontext.text: Consider pdf \(f(x|\theta)=\dfrac{e^{(x-\theta)}}{[1+e^{(x-\theta)}]^2}\) (logistic). Based on one observation \(X\), a UMP test of size \(\alpha\) for \(H_0:\theta\leq\theta_0\) vs \(H_1:\theta>\theta_0\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Reject \(H_0\) if \(X>k\) where \(k\) satisfies \(\alpha=P_{\theta_0}[X>k]\)

The logistic distribution has monotone likelihood ratio in \(x\), so by the Neyman-Pearson lemma the UMP test is of the form "reject if \(X>c\)" where \(c\) is determined by the size condition \(\alpha=P_{\theta_0}(X>c)\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q55 - Expected value of Wilcoxon rank-sum statistic

questiontext.text: Consider 35 i.i.d. observations: \(X_1,\ldots,X_{15}\) and \(Y_1,\ldots,Y_{20}\). Let \(R\) be the Wilcoxon rank-sum statistic based on the ranks of the \(X\)'s in the combined sample of 35. Then \(E(R)=\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q56 - Estimability in two-way ANOVA

questiontext.text: Let \(I,J\geq 5\). In two-way ANOVA \(y_{ij}=\alpha+\beta_i+\gamma_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\) with \(\sum\beta_i=\sum\gamma_j=0\). Which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\beta_1-\beta_2\) is estimable

In two-way ANOVA without interaction, individual effects \(\beta_i, \gamma_j\) are not estimable due to non-identifiability, but contrasts like \(\beta_1-\beta_2\) are estimable (they are linear combinations of estimable functions).

name.text: NOV2020 Q57 - Central chi-squared distribution from bivariate normal

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,X_2\) be i.i.d. \(p\times 1\) multivariate normal with mean \(\mu\) and positive definite covariance \(\Sigma\). Which random variable always has a central \(\chi^2\) distribution?

name.text: NOV2020 Q58 - Unbiased estimate of population variance (DROPPED)

questiontext.text: [DROPPED by NTA] Ten units chosen by SRSWOR from a population of 100. Sample variance \(s^2=\frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}(y_i-\bar{y})^2\). An unbiased estimate of the population variance \(\sigma^2=\frac{1}{100}\sum_{i=1}^{100}(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (Dropped by NTA)

For SRSWOR with \(n=10\), \(N=100\): The sample variance \(s^2=(1/n)\sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2\) relates to population variance \(\sigma^2\). An unbiased estimate is \(\hat{\sigma}^2=\frac{n}{n-1}\cdot\frac{N-1}{N}\cdot s^2=\frac{10}{9}\cdot\frac{99}{100}\cdot s^2\). This question was officially dropped; all candidates received full marks.

answer.text: \(\dfrac{10}{9}\cdot\dfrac{99}{100}\cdot s^2\) (question dropped; answer not officially given)

name.text: NOV2020 Q59 - Randomized block design uncorrelated contrasts

questiontext.text: In a Randomized Block Design with \(b\) blocks and \(k\) treatments (\(y_{ij}=\alpha+\beta_i+\gamma_j+\varepsilon_{ij}\)), which is true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Estimate of any treatment contrast is uncorrelated with estimate of any block contrast

In RBD, treatment effects \(\beta_i\) and block effects \(\gamma_j\) are orthogonal. Hence estimates of treatment contrasts and block contrasts are uncorrelated. The error SS has \((b-1)(k-1)\) df, not \(bk-1\).

answer.text: Estimates of any two treatment contrasts are uncorrelated
Error SS has \(bk-1\) degrees of freedom
Estimate of any treatment contrast is uncorrelated with estimate of any block contrast
Correlation between estimates of two treatment contrasts is always negative

name.text: NOV2020 Q60 - Maximum and minimum of linear function over L1 ball

questiontext.text: The maximum and minimum values of \(5x+7y\) subject to \(|x|+|y|\leq 1\) are

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 7 and \(-7\)

The constraint \(|x|+|y|\leq 1\) is the \(\ell^1\) ball. The maximum of a linear function over the \(\ell^1\) ball is the \(\ell^\infty\) norm of the coefficient vector: \(\max(|5|,|7|)=7\), achieved at \((x,y)=(0,1)\). Minimum is \(-7\) at \((0,-1)\).

info.text: CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences, 26 November 2020, Shift 2. Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q61-120). Multiple correct answers (MSQ). Marks: +4.75, no negative marking. Attempt any 20.

name.text: NOV2020 Q61 - Sets in bijection with R

questiontext.text:
Which of the following sets are in bijection with \(\mathbb{R}\)? (Select all correct answers)

(1) Set of all maps from \(\{0,1\}\) to \(\mathbb{N}\)
(2) Set of all maps from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\{0,1\}\)
(3) Set of all subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\)
(4) Set of all subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) Maps \(\mathbb{N}\to\{0,1\}\) correspond to characteristic functions of subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\), i.e., \(|\{0,1\}^\mathbb{N}|=2^{\aleph_0}=|\mathbb{R}|\). (3) \(|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})|=2^{\aleph_0}=|\mathbb{R}|\). (1) Maps \(\{0,1\}\to\mathbb{N}\) are countable. (4) \(|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})|=2^{2^{\aleph_0}}>|\mathbb{R}|\).

answer.text: Set of all maps from \(\{0,1\}\) to \(\mathbb{N}\)
Set of all maps from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\{0,1\}\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q62 - Convergence of series

questiontext.text:
Which of the following statements are true? (Select all correct answers)

(1) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n}{\sqrt{n}}\) is convergent
(2) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n}{\sqrt{n}+n}\) is absolutely convergent
(3) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{[1+(-1)^n]\sqrt{n}+\log n}{n^{3/2}}\) is convergent
(4) \(\displaystyle\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n\sqrt{n}+1}{n^{3/2}}\) is convergent

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

(1) Alternating series with \(1/\sqrt{n}\to 0\) monotonically: convergent by AST. (2) \(\sum\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}+n}\approx\sum\frac{1}{n}\) diverges, so not absolutely convergent. (3) When \(n\) is odd: terms involve \(\log n/n^{3/2}\) which converge, but even terms give \(2\sqrt{n}/n^{3/2}=2/n\), divergent. (4) \(\sum(-1)^n/n\) converges (AST) and \(\sum 1/n^{3/2}\) converges: sum converges.

name.text: NOV2020 Q63 - Continuity of functions defined via series

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{2xy}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\), and \(g(x,y)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{f(x-n,y-n)}{2^n}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The function \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\) for all \(c\)
(2) \(g\) is continuous from \(\mathbb{R}^2\) into \(\mathbb{R}\)
(3) \(g\) is not a well-defined function
(4) \(g\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{(k,k):k\in\mathbb{N}\}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

\(f\) is bounded (\(|f|\leq 1\)) and continuous except at \((0,0)\). The series defining \(g\) converges absolutely (by \(\sum 1/2^n\)). \(g\) is well-defined everywhere. \(g\) is discontinuous at points \((k,k)\) where \(f(\cdot)\) has a discontinuity. For fixed \(c\), \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous since the discontinuities of each \(f(x-n,y-n)\) occur at \(y=c-n\neq c\) (for fixed \(c\neq n\))... (1) and (4) are correct.

answer.text: The function \(h(y)=g(c,y)\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\) for all \(c\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q64 - Pointwise and uniform convergence of power series

questiontext.text:
Consider \(A(x)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n(1-x)\) and \(B(x)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty(-1)^nx^n(1-x)\), \(x\in[0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Both \(A(x)\) and \(B(x)\) converge pointwise on \([0,1]\)
(2) Both \(A(x)\) and \(B(x)\) converge uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(3) \(A(x)\) converges uniformly but \(B(x)\) does not
(4) \(B(x)\) converges uniformly but \(A(x)\) does not

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (4)

For \(x\in[0,1)\): \(A(x)=\sum x^n(1-x)=1\) (geometric series). At \(x=1\): \(A(1)=0\). So \(A\to 1\) for \(x\in[0,1)\) but \(A(1)=0\): pointwise limit is discontinuous, so convergence is NOT uniform. For \(B(x)\): \(B(x)=\sum(-x)^n(1-x)=\frac{1-x}{1+x}\) for \(x\in[0,1)\), \(B(1)=0\). This limit \(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\) is continuous at \(x=1\), and uniform convergence holds. Both converge pointwise.

name.text: NOV2020 Q65 - Convergence of improper integral with power of t

questiontext.text:
For \(p\in\mathbb{R}\), consider the improper integral \(I_p=\displaystyle\int_0^1 t^p\sin t\,dt\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(I_p\) is convergent for \(p=-1/2\)
(2) \(I_p\) is divergent for \(p=-3/2\)
(3) \(I_p\) is convergent for \(p=4/3\)
(4) \(I_p\) is divergent for \(p=-4/3\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

Near \(t=0\): \(\sin t\approx t\), so \(t^p\sin t\approx t^{p+1}\). The integral \(\int_0^1 t^{p+1}\,dt\) converges iff \(p+1>-1\), i.e., \(p>-2\). (1) \(p=-1/2>-2\): convergent ✓. (2) \(p=-3/2>-2\): convergent, so divergent is FALSE... Wait, \(-3/2>-2\), so it should converge. But the answer says ALL FOUR are correct.

Re-checking: \(I_p=\int_0^1 t^p\sin t\,dt\). \(|t^p\sin t|\leq t^{p+1}\) near 0. Converges iff \(p+1>-1\Rightarrow p>-2\). All given values: \(-1/2, -3/2, 4/3, -4/3\) are greater than \(-2\). So (1),(3) converge. (2),(4): \(p=-3/2,-4/3>-2\) so also converge. But question says (2),(4) diverge — NTA answer marks all four as correct.

name.text: NOV2020 Q66 - Sequence converging uniformly on compact subsets

questiontext.text:
Suppose \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence of real-valued functions on \(\mathbb{R}\) converging to a continuous function \(f\) uniformly on each closed bounded subset of \(\mathbb{R}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\{f_n\}\) converges to \(f\) uniformly on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) \(\{f_n\}\) converges to \(f\) pointwise on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(3) For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is bounded
(4) For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) only

(2) Pointwise convergence follows immediately since any point \(x\) is in the closed bounded set \([x-1,x+1]\). (1) Is false: uniform convergence on each compact set does not imply uniform convergence on all of \(\mathbb{R}\). (3),(4) Not necessarily: \(f_n\) may be unbounded or discontinuous.

answer.text: For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is bounded
For all sufficiently large \(n\), \(f_n\) is continuous

name.text: NOV2020 Q67 - Uniform continuity of exponential functions

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x)=e^{-x}\) and \(g(x)=e^{-x^2}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Both \(f\) and \(g\) are uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) \(f\) is uniformly continuous on every interval \([a,+\infty)\), \(a\in\mathbb{R}\)
(3) \(g\) is uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(4) \(f(x)g(x)\) is uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) \(f'(x)=-e^{-x}\) is bounded on \([a,\infty)\) by \(e^{-a}\), so \(f\) is Lipschitz, hence uniformly continuous. (3) \(g'(x)=-2xe^{-x^2}\to 0\) as \(|x|\to\infty\), so \(g'\) is bounded on \(\mathbb{R}\), giving uniform continuity. (1) \(f=e^{-x}\) is NOT uniformly continuous on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) (unbounded derivative for \(x\to-\infty\)). (4) \(f(x)g(x)=e^{-x-x^2}\) is not uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\).

answer.text: Both \(f\) and \(g\) are uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
\(f\) is uniformly continuous on every interval \([a,+\infty)\), \(a\in\mathbb{R}\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q68 - Continuity and differentiability of f(x,y)=x^3/(x^2+y^2)

questiontext.text:
Define \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{x^3}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is discontinuous at \((0,0)\)
(2) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\)
(3) All directional derivatives of \(f\) at \((0,0)\) exist
(4) \(f\) is not differentiable at \((0,0)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2), (3), and (4)

(2) \(|f(x,y)|\leq\frac{|x|^3}{x^2+y^2}\leq|x|\to 0\): continuous at \((0,0)\). (3) Directional derivative in direction \((h,k)\): \(\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(th,tk)}{t}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{t^3h^3}{t^2(h^2+k^2)}\cdot\frac{1}{t}=\frac{h^3}{h^2+k^2}\): exists for all \((h,k)\neq 0\). (4) If \(f\) were differentiable, directional derivatives would be linear in \((h,k)\), but \(h^3/(h^2+k^2)\) is not linear.

answer.text: All directional derivatives of \(f\) at \((0,0)\) exist

name.text: NOV2020 Q69 - Properties of f(x,y)=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)

questiontext.text:
Define \(f(x,y)=\dfrac{x^2-y^2}{x^2+y^2}\) for \((x,y)\neq(0,0)\), \(f(0,0)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\)
(2) \(f\) is bounded in a neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
(3) \(f\) is not bounded in any neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
(4) \(f\) has all directional derivatives at \((0,0)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (4)

(2) \(|f(x,y)|=\frac{|x^2-y^2|}{x^2+y^2}\leq 1\) everywhere: bounded. (4) Directional derivative in direction \((h,k)\): \(\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(th,tk)}{t}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{h^2-k^2}{h^2+k^2}\cdot\frac{1}{t}\). This limit is 0 only if \(h^2=k^2\), otherwise diverges... Actually \(\frac{d}{dt}f(th,tk)\big|_{t=0}=\lim_{t\to 0}\frac{(h^2-k^2)/(h^2+k^2)}{t}\) which only exists and equals 0 when \(h=\pm k\). So (4) is debatable. NTA key: (2) and (4). (1) \(f(x,0)=1\neq 0=f(0,0)\): discontinuous.

answer.text: \(f\) is bounded in a neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)
\(f\) is not bounded in any neighbourhood of \((0,0)\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q70 - Properties of a piecewise-defined function p on R^2

questiontext.text:
Let \(p:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\) be defined by \(p(x,y)=|x|\) if \(x\neq 0\), and \(p(0,y)=|y|\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(p(x,y)=0\) iff \(x=y=0\)
(2) \(p(x,y)\geq 0\) for all \(x,y\)
(3) \(p(\alpha x,\alpha y)=|\alpha|p(x,y)\) for all \(\alpha\in\mathbb{R}\)
(4) \(p(x_1+x_2,y_1+y_2)\leq p(x_1,y_1)+p(x_2,y_2)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(p(0,y)=|y|=0\Rightarrow y=0\); \(p(x,y)=|x|=0\Rightarrow x=0\). Correct. (2) Obviously \(p\geq 0\). (3) If \(\alpha x\neq 0\): \(p(\alpha x,\alpha y)=|\alpha x|=|\alpha||x|=|\alpha|p(x,y)\). If \(\alpha x=0\): \(p(0,\alpha y)=|\alpha y|=|\alpha||y|=|\alpha|p(0,y)\). Correct. (4) Fails: take \((x_1,y_1)=(1,0)\), \((x_2,y_2)=(-1,1)\). Then \(p(0,1)=1\) but \(p(1,0)+p(-1,1)=1+1=2\). Wait, triangle inequality holds here. Actually take \((1,1)+(-1,2)=(0,3)\): \(p(0,3)=3\leq 1+1=2\)? No, \(3>2\): triangle inequality fails!

name.text: NOV2020 Q71 - Properties of idempotent matrix

questiontext.text:
Let \(P\) be a square matrix with \(P^2=P\) (idempotent/projection). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Trace of \(P\) is an irrational number
(2) Trace of \(P\) = rank of \(P\)
(3) Trace of \(P\) is an integer
(4) Trace of \(P\) is an imaginary complex number

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

For an idempotent matrix \(P^2=P\): eigenvalues are 0 or 1. \(\mathrm{tr}(P)=\) number of eigenvalues equal to 1 = number of 1's in Jordan form = \(\mathrm{rank}(P)\). Hence trace equals rank (a non-negative integer).

answer.text: Trace of \(P\) is an irrational number
Trace of \(P\) is an imaginary complex number

name.text: NOV2020 Q72 - Block matrix eigenvalues

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) and \(B\) be \(n\times n\) real matrices and \(C=\begin{pmatrix}A&B\\B&A\end{pmatrix}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A+B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(2) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A-B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(3) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) or \(B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
(4) All eigenvalues of \(C\) are real

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

Block diagonalise using \(P=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix}I&I\\I&-I\end{pmatrix}\): \(P^{-1}CP=\begin{pmatrix}A+B&0\\0&A-B\end{pmatrix}\). So eigenvalues of \(C\) are eigenvalues of \(A+B\) and eigenvalues of \(A-B\). (1),(2) follow directly. (3) Not generally true. (4) Not necessarily real if \(A,B\) are complex.

answer.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A+B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A-B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)
If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) or \(B\), then \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(C\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q73 - Properties when Ax=b has no solution

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be \(n\times n\) real and \(b\) be \(n\times 1\). Suppose \(Ax=b\) has no solution. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\exists\) \(n\times 1\) vector \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has a unique solution
(2) \(\exists\) infinitely many vectors \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has no solution
(3) If \(y\) is the first column of \(A\), then \(Ax=y\) has a unique solution
(4) \(\det A=0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (4)

Since \(Ax=b\) has no solution, \(A\) is singular: \(\det A=0\) (4). The column space of \(A\) is a proper subspace. (2) All \(c\notin\mathrm{col}(A)\) give no solution, and there are infinitely many such \(c\). (1) Since \(\det A=0\), every consistent system has infinitely many solutions, NOT a unique solution... Actually (1) may be false too. NTA answer: (1),(2),(4).

answer.text: \(\exists\) vector \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has a unique solution
\(\exists\) infinitely many \(c\) such that \(Ax=c\) has no solution
If \(y\) is the first column of \(A\), then \(Ax=y\) has a unique solution

name.text: NOV2020 Q74 - Rank given linearly independent rows and columns

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be an \(n\times n\) matrix such that the first 3 rows are linearly independent and the first 5 columns are linearly independent. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(A\) has at least 5 linearly independent rows
(2) \(3\leq\mathrm{rank}(A)\leq 5\)
(3) \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\geq 5\)
(4) \(\mathrm{rank}(A^2)\geq 5\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) only

3 linearly independent rows \(\Rightarrow\) rank \(\geq 3\). 5 linearly independent columns \(\Rightarrow\) rank \(\geq 5\). But row rank = column rank, so rank \(\geq 5\)... (3) should also be true. However NTA answer is (2). The 5 columns being independent means rank \(\geq 5\), so the lower bound is 5, giving exactly rank \(\geq 5\). The upper bound from rows: rank \(\leq n\). NTA key: \(3\leq\mathrm{rank}(A)\leq 5\)... but 5 independent columns forces rank \(\geq 5\). We accept NTA answer (2).

answer.text: \(A\) has at least 5 linearly independent rows

name.text: NOV2020 Q75 - Infimum and supremum of inner product

questiontext.text:
Let \(n\) be a positive integer, \(F\) a non-empty proper subset of \(\{1,\ldots,n\}\). Define \(\langle x,y\rangle_F=\sum_{k\in F}x_ky_k\) and \(T=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n:\langle x,x\rangle_F=0\}\). For \(y\in\mathbb{R}^n\), \(y\neq 0\), which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\inf_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(2) \(\sup_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(3) \(\inf_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F<\langle y,y\rangle_F\)
(4) \(\sup_{x\in T}\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F>\langle y,y\rangle_F\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

\(T=\{x:x_k=0\text{ for all }k\in F\}\). For \(x\in T\): \(\langle x+y,x+y\rangle_F=\langle x,x\rangle_F+2\langle x,y\rangle_F+\langle y,y\rangle_F=0+0+\langle y,y\rangle_F=\langle y,y\rangle_F\). The value is constant on \(T\), so both inf and sup equal \(\langle y,y\rangle_F\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q76 - Properties of right shift operator on l^2

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:\ell^2\to\ell^2\) be defined by \(T(x_1,x_2,x_3,\ldots)=(0,x_1,x_2,x_3,\ldots)\) (right shift). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\|T\|=1\)
(2) \(T\) is injective
(3) \(T\) is not surjective
(4) \(T^*T=I\) (identity)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) \(\|Tx\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x\), so \(\|T\|=1\). (2) \(Tx=0\Rightarrow x=0\): injective. (3) \((1,0,0,\ldots)\notin\mathrm{range}(T)\): not surjective. (4) \(T^*\) is the left shift \(T^*(y_1,y_2,\ldots)=(y_2,y_3,\ldots)\), and \(T^*T(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=T^*(0,x_1,x_2,\ldots)=(x_1,x_2,\ldots)=Ix\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q77 - Properties of infinite-dimensional Banach space

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be an infinite-dimensional Banach space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The closed unit ball of \(X\) is not compact
(2) \(X\) contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence
(3) \(X^*\) (dual space) is a Banach space
(4) \(X\) is reflexive

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) By Riesz's lemma, the closed unit ball of an infinite-dimensional normed space is never compact. (2) Follows from (1): the unit ball contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence. (3) The dual of any normed space is a Banach space (completeness of bounded linear functionals). (4) Not all Banach spaces are reflexive (e.g., \(\ell^1\)).

answer.text: The closed unit ball of \(X\) is not compact
\(X\) contains a sequence with no convergent subsequence

name.text: NOV2020 Q78 - Properties of periodic continuous function

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function satisfying \(f(x+1)=f(x)\) for all \(x\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is bounded
(2) \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(3) \(f\) is differentiable
(4) \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx\) converges

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(f\) continuous on \([0,1]\) (compact), so bounded; periodicity extends this to all of \(\mathbb{R}\). (2) \(f\) continuous on compact \([0,2]\) is uniformly continuous; periodicity gives uniform continuity on \(\mathbb{R}\). (3) Not necessarily differentiable (e.g., \(f(x)=|\sin(\pi x)|\) is non-differentiable at integers). (4) \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty f\,dx\) diverges unless \(f\equiv 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q79 - Entire function bounded by sqrt|z|

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}\) be an entire function satisfying \(|f(z)|\leq M|z|^{1/2}\) for all \(z\). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f(z)=\sqrt{z}\)
(2) \(f\) is a non-constant polynomial
(3) \(f\) is unbounded
(4) \(f\equiv 0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) \(f\equiv 0\)

By Cauchy's estimates on a disk of radius \(R\): for the \(n\)-th derivative, \(|f^{(n)}(0)|\leq\frac{n!MR^{1/2}}{R^n}=\frac{n!M}{R^{n-1/2}}\to 0\) as \(R\to\infty\) for \(n\geq 1\). So all derivatives at 0 vanish, meaning \(f\) is constant. Since \(|f(z)|\leq M|z|^{1/2}\to 0\) as \(z\to 0\), \(f\equiv 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q80 - Dense image of non-constant entire function

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}\) be entire and non-constant. Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) is bounded
(2) \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\)
(3) \(f\) has no zeros
(4) \(f\) assumes every value exactly once

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\)

By Liouville's theorem, \(f\) is unbounded. By Picard's little theorem, a non-constant entire function takes every complex value, with at most one exception. Hence \(f(\mathbb{C})\) is dense (in fact, the image omits at most one value). (1) False by Liouville. (3) Not necessarily. (4) Not necessarily (polynomials repeat values).

name.text: NOV2020 Q81 - Product of holomorphic functions equals zero

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) and \(g\) be holomorphic in a connected open set \(\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{C}\) and \(f\cdot g\equiv 0\) on \(\Omega\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\equiv 0\) or \(g\equiv 0\) on \(\Omega\)
(2) If \(f\not\equiv 0\) and \(g\not\equiv 0\), then \(\Omega\) has no isolated zeros
(3) \(f+g\equiv 0\)
(4) \(f=g\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) Holomorphic functions on a connected domain form an integral domain: if \(fg=0\) and \(f\not\equiv 0\), then the zero set of \(f\) is discrete (isolated), but \(g\) must be zero on an open set, forcing \(g\equiv 0\) by the identity theorem. (2) This is vacuously correct given (1): if both are non-zero, contradiction.

answer.text: If \(f\not\equiv 0\) and \(g\not\equiv 0\), then \(\Omega\) has no isolated zeros

name.text: NOV2020 Q82 - Singularity when Re(f) is non-negative

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) have an isolated singularity at \(z=0\). If \(\mathrm{Re}\,f(z)\geq 0\) in a punctured neighbourhood of \(z=0\), which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(z=0\) is an essential singularity
(2) \(z=0\) is a pole of order \(\geq 2\)
(3) \(z=0\) is a removable singularity
(4) \(f\) is not analytic near 0

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(z=0\) is a removable singularity

Consider \(h(z)=e^{-f(z)}\). Then \(|h(z)|=e^{-\mathrm{Re}\,f(z)}\leq 1\), so \(h\) is bounded near 0 and analytic. By Riemann's removable singularity theorem, \(h\) extends analytically through 0. This forces \(f\) to also have a removable singularity (since \(f=-\log h\) locally, and \(h(0)\neq 0\)).

name.text: NOV2020 Q83 - Meromorphic function with poles at 0 and 1

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) be meromorphic in \(\mathbb{C}\) with poles only at \(z=0\) and \(z=1\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) can be written as \(f=h+p+q\) where \(h\) is entire, \(p\) has singularity only at 0, \(q\) only at 1
(2) \(f\) has at most finitely many zeros in any compact set
(3) \(f\) has finitely many poles total
(4) \(f\) is rational

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) By Mittag-Leffler decomposition: any meromorphic function can be decomposed as partial fractions plus entire part. (2) Zeros of a meromorphic (non-zero) function are isolated, so finitely many in any compact set. (3) False: \(f\) could have essential singularities or infinitely many poles accumulating at 0 or 1. (4) Not necessarily rational.

answer.text: \(f=h+p+q\) where \(h\) entire, \(p\) singular only at 0, \(q\) singular only at 1
\(f\) has at most finitely many zeros in any compact set

name.text: NOV2020 Q84 - Group of order 12

questiontext.text:
Let \(G\) be a group of order 12. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(G\) has a subgroup of order 4
(2) \(G\) has a subgroup of order 3
(3) \(G\) is abelian
(4) \(G\cong A_4\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) By Sylow's theorem, \(G\) has a Sylow 2-subgroup of order 4. (2) By Cauchy's theorem (or Sylow), \(G\) has an element (and subgroup) of order 3. (3) Not all groups of order 12 are abelian (e.g., \(A_4\), \(D_6\)). (4) Not all groups of order 12 are isomorphic to \(A_4\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q85 - Ring Z[sqrt(-5)] properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(R=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(R\) is an integral domain
(2) \(2\) is irreducible but not prime in \(R\)
(3) \(R\) is a UFD
(4) \(R\) is a PID

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(R\subseteq\mathbb{C}\) is a subring; no zero divisors, so integral domain. (2) The factorisation \(6=2\times 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\) shows two distinct irreducible factorisations, so \(R\) is NOT a UFD. Element 2 is irreducible (norm argument) but \(2\nmid(1+\sqrt{-5})\) even though \(2\mid N(1+\sqrt{-5})=6\), so 2 is not prime. (3),(4) False since \(R\) is not a UFD.

answer.text: 2 is irreducible but not prime in \(R\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q86 - Properties of contraction mapping

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a metric space and \(f:X\to X\) a contraction mapping. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f\) has at most one fixed point
(2) If \(X\) is complete, \(f\) has exactly one fixed point
(3) \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(4) Iterates \(f^n(x_0)\) converge for any \(x_0\in X\), if \(X\) is complete

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) If \(f(x)=x\) and \(f(y)=y\), then \(d(x,y)=d(f(x),f(y))\leq cd(x,y)\) with \(c<1\), so \(d(x,y)=0\), \(x=y\). (2) Banach fixed-point theorem. (3) \(d(f(x),f(y))\leq cd(x,y)\) shows Lipschitz continuity, hence uniform continuity. (4) The iterates form a Cauchy sequence and converge to the fixed point.

answer.text: \(f\) has at most one fixed point
If \(X\) is complete, \(f\) has exactly one fixed point
Iterates \(f^n(x_0)\) converge for any \(x_0\in X\), if \(X\) is complete

name.text: NOV2020 Q87 - LLN, CLT, and consistency of sample variance

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1]=\mu\), \(\mathrm{Var}(X_1)=\sigma^2<\infty\). Let \(\bar{X}_n=\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\) and \(S_n^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X}_n)^2\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bar{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu\)
(2) \(S_n^2\xrightarrow{P}\sigma^2\)
(3) \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n-\mu)/\sigma\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\)
(4) \(\bar{X}_n\) is always the MLE of \(\mu\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Weak Law of Large Numbers. (2) \(S_n^2\) is unbiased and consistent for \(\sigma^2\). (3) Central Limit Theorem. (4) False: MLE depends on the distribution (e.g., for Cauchy, MLE is the sample median, not mean).

name.text: NOV2020 Q88 - Independence in bivariate normal distribution

questiontext.text:
Let \(X=(X_1,X_2)^T\sim N_2(\mu,\Sigma)\) where \(\Sigma=\begin{pmatrix}\sigma_1^2&\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2\\\rho\sigma_1\sigma_2&\sigma_2^2\end{pmatrix}\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(X_1+X_2\) and \(X_1-X_2\) are independent iff \(\sigma_1=\sigma_2\)
(2) \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) are independent iff \(\rho=0\)
(3) \(\rho=0\Rightarrow X_1,X_2\) uncorrelated only (not independent)
(4) \(X_1|X_2=x_2\) is not normal

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(\mathrm{Cov}(X_1+X_2,X_1-X_2)=\sigma_1^2-\sigma_2^2\). This is zero iff \(\sigma_1=\sigma_2\); under bivariate normality, zero covariance implies independence. (2) For bivariate normal, uncorrelated (\(\rho=0\)) implies independent. (3) False: for bivariate normal, \(\rho=0\) implies independence. (4) False: conditional distribution \(X_1|X_2=x_2\) is normal.

name.text: NOV2020 Q89 - OLS estimator in regression through origin

questiontext.text:
Let \((X_1,Y_1),\ldots,(X_n,Y_n)\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1^2]<\infty\) and \(E[Y_1^2]<\infty\). Let \(\hat{\beta}\) be the OLS estimator of \(\beta\) in \(Y=\beta X\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\hat{\beta}=\dfrac{\sum X_iY_i}{\sum X_i^2}\)
(2) \(\hat{\beta}\xrightarrow{P}\dfrac{E[XY]}{E[X^2]}\)
(3) \(\hat{\beta}\) is always unbiased
(4) \(\hat{\beta}\) is the MLE under all distributions

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) OLS minimises \(\sum(Y_i-\beta X_i)^2\): derivative gives \(\hat{\beta}=\sum X_iY_i/\sum X_i^2\). (2) By LLN, \(\hat{\beta}\to E[XY]/E[X^2]\) in probability. (3) Unbiased only when \(E[Y|X]=\beta X\); not always. (4) MLE depends on the error distribution assumption.

answer.text: \(\hat{\beta}\) is the MLE under all distributions

name.text: NOV2020 Q90 - MLE, completeness, and UMVUE for Bernoulli

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. \(\mathrm{Bernoulli}(\theta)\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bar{X}\) is the MLE of \(\theta\)
(2) \(\bar{X}\) is a complete sufficient statistic
(3) \(\bar{X}\) is the UMVUE of \(\theta\)
(4) \(\bar{X}^2\) is the UMVUE of \(\theta^2\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Likelihood is maximised at \(\hat{\theta}=\bar{X}\). (2) \(T=\sum X_i\) (equivalently \(\bar{X}\)) is complete sufficient for the Bernoulli exponential family. (3) Since \(\bar{X}\) is an unbiased function of the complete sufficient statistic, it is the UMVUE. (4) \(\bar{X}^2\) is biased for \(\theta^2\); the UMVUE of \(\theta^2\) is \(\frac{n\bar{X}^2-\bar{X}}{n-1}\) (using Rao-Blackwell).

name.text: NOV2020 Q91 - UMP unbiased two-sided test

questiontext.text:
For testing \(H_0:\mu=0\) vs \(H_1:\mu\neq 0\) where \(\bar{X}\) is the sample mean of \(n\) i.i.d. \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known. The UMP unbiased level \(\alpha\) test rejects \(H_0\) if

(1) \(|\bar{X}|>z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)
(2) \(|Z|>z_{\alpha/2}\) where \(Z=\sqrt{n}\bar{X}/\sigma\)
(3) \(\bar{X}>z_\alpha\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)
(4) \(\bar{X}^2>\chi^2_{\alpha,1}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

The two-sided UMP unbiased test for normal mean rejects when \(|Z|>z_{\alpha/2}\), where \(Z=\sqrt{n}\bar{X}/\sigma\). Options (1) and (2) are equivalent expressions of this rejection region.

name.text: NOV2020 Q92 - Pointwise vs uniform convergence of f_n(x)=nx(1-x^2)^n

questiontext.text:
Let \(f_n(x)=nx(1-x^2)^n\) on \([0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f_n\to 0\) pointwise on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx\not\to 0\)
(3) \(f_n\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(4) \(\sup_n\|f_n\|_\infty<\infty\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) For \(x\in(0,1]\): \(|f_n(x)|=nx(1-x^2)^n\to 0\) since \((1-x^2)^n\to 0\) exponentially. At \(x=0\): \(f_n(0)=0\). Pointwise \(\to 0\). (2) \(\int_0^1 nx(1-x^2)^n\,dx=\frac{n}{2(n+1)}\to\frac{1}{2}\neq 0\). Not uniformly convergent. (3) False: max at \(x^*=1/\sqrt{2n+1}\) gives \(\|f_n\|_\infty\approx n^{1/2}/(2e^{1/2})\to\infty\). (4) False: \(\|f_n\|_\infty\to\infty\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q93 - Convergence of f_n(x)=x/(1+n^2x^2)

questiontext.text:
Let \(f_n(x)=\dfrac{x}{1+n^2x^2}\) on \([0,1]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(f_n\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(f_n'\to 0\) pointwise on \((0,1]\)
(3) \(f_n'\to 0\) uniformly on \([0,1]\)
(4) \(\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx=0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(|f_n(x)|\leq\frac{1}{2n}\to 0\) uniformly (AM-GM: \(1+n^2x^2\geq 2n|x|\)). (2) \(f_n'(x)=\frac{1-n^2x^2}{(1+n^2x^2)^2}\to 0\) for \(x>0\) (numerator \(\to-n^2x^2\), denominator \(\to n^4x^4\), ratio \(\to 0\)). (3) \(f_n'(0)=1\neq 0\), so not uniform. (4) \(\int_0^1\frac{x}{1+n^2x^2}\,dx=\frac{1}{2n^2}\ln(1+n^2)\neq 0\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q94 - Properties of integral function F(x)=integral of f

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\in L^1([0,1])\). Define \(F(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(F\) is absolutely continuous on \([0,1]\)
(2) \(F'=f\) a.e.
(3) \(F\) is uniformly continuous
(4) \(F\) is differentiable everywhere

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(F\) is absolutely continuous as an indefinite integral of an \(L^1\) function. (2) Lebesgue's differentiation theorem: \(F'=f\) a.e. (3) Absolute continuity implies uniform continuity. (4) Not everywhere: \(F\) may fail to be differentiable on a set of measure zero where \(f\) has jumps.

name.text: NOV2020 Q95 - L1 and L2 convergence in measure spaces

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,\mathcal{B},\mu)\) be a measure space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\), then \(\int_E f_n\to\int_E f\) for all measurable \(E\)
(2) If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^2\) and \(\mu(X)<\infty\), then \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\)
(3) Convergence in measure implies \(L^1\) convergence
(4) \(f_n\to f\) a.e. implies \(L^1\) convergence

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(|\int_E f_n-\int_E f|\leq\int_E|f_n-f|\leq\|f_n-f\|_1\to 0\). (2) By Cauchy-Schwarz: \(\|f_n-f\|_1\leq\sqrt{\mu(X)}\|f_n-f\|_2\to 0\). (3) False: convergence in measure does NOT imply \(L^1\) convergence. (4) False: a.e. convergence does not imply \(L^1\) (need dominated convergence or equi-integrability).

answer.text: If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\), then \(\int_E f_n\to\int_E f\) for all measurable \(E\)
If \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^2\) and \(\mu(X)<\infty\), then \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q96 - Orthogonal complement properties in inner product space

questiontext.text:
Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space and \(W\subseteq V\) a subspace. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\)
(2) \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\)
(3) \(\dim W+\dim W^\perp=\dim V\)
(4) The orthogonal projection onto \(W\) is uniquely defined

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

In finite dimensions: (1) Every vector decomposes uniquely as \(w+w^\perp\). (2) \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\) in finite dimensions. (3) Dimension theorem for orthogonal complement. (4) The orthogonal projection is the unique best approximation operator onto \(W\).

answer.text: The orthogonal projection onto \(W\) is uniquely defined

name.text: NOV2020 Q97 - Eigenvalues of linear operator on complex space

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:V\to V\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional complex vector space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The characteristic polynomial of \(T\) splits into linear factors over \(\mathbb{C}\)
(2) \(T\) has at least one eigenvalue
(3) If \(T\) is self-adjoint, all eigenvalues are real
(4) \(T\) is always diagonalisable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed: every polynomial factors completely. (2) Since the characteristic polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\). (3) For self-adjoint (Hermitian) operators, all eigenvalues are real. (4) Not every operator is diagonalisable (e.g., Jordan blocks).

answer.text: The characteristic polynomial of \(T\) splits into linear factors over \(\mathbb{C}\)
If \(T\) is self-adjoint, all eigenvalues are real

name.text: NOV2020 Q98 - Measurability of unions and intersections

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{E_\alpha\}\) be an arbitrary collection of Lebesgue measurable sets in \((\mathbb{R},\mathcal{B},\lambda)\). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable
(2) \(\bigcap_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
(3) \(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
(4) Uncountable unions of measurable sets are measurable

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

The \(\sigma\)-algebra is closed under countable unions and intersections. (2) Countable intersection of measurable sets is measurable. (3) Countable union of measurable sets is measurable. (1) Uncountable unions are NOT necessarily measurable. (4) Same reason: uncountable unions can fail.

answer.text: \(\bigcap_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
\(\bigcup_\alpha E_\alpha\) is measurable if the collection is countable
Uncountable unions of measurable sets are measurable

name.text: NOV2020 Q99 - MLE, UMVUE, and sufficiency for Poisson

questiontext.text:
Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be i.i.d. \(\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\sum X_i\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(n\lambda)\)
(2) \(\bar{X}\) is the MLE of \(\lambda\)
(3) \(\bar{X}\) is the UMVUE of \(\lambda\)
(4) \(\bar{X}\) is not a sufficient statistic

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Sum of independent Poissons: \(\sum X_i\sim\text{Poisson}(n\lambda)\). (2) MLE for Poisson is \(\hat{\lambda}=\bar{X}\). (3) \(\bar{X}\) is unbiased and function of complete sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\), hence UMVUE. (4) False: \(\bar{X}\) (or \(\sum X_i\)) is sufficient.

name.text: NOV2020 Q100 - Continuous functions preserve compactness and connectedness

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a continuous map between metric spaces. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) If \(X\) is compact, \(f\) is uniformly continuous
(2) \(f\) maps compact sets to compact sets
(3) \(f\) maps connected sets to connected sets
(4) \(f\) maps closed sets to closed sets

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Continuous function on a compact metric space is uniformly continuous (Heine-Cantor). (2) Continuous image of compact set is compact. (3) Continuous image of connected set is connected. (4) False: continuous functions do not generally preserve closed sets (e.g., projection).

answer.text: If \(X\) is compact, \(f\) is uniformly continuous
\(f\) maps compact sets to compact sets
\(f\) maps connected sets to connected sets
\(f\) maps closed sets to closed sets

name.text: NOV2020 Q101 - Properties of map with eventually contraction

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a complete metric space and \(T:X\to X\) satisfy \(d(T^nx,T^ny)\leq c^n d(x,y)\) for some \(c<1\) and all \(n\geq 1\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(T\) has a unique fixed point
(2) \(T^n(x_0)\) converges to the fixed point for any \(x_0\)
(3) \(T\) is necessarily a contraction
(4) \(T\) is continuous

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (4)

(1) The iterates form a Cauchy sequence since \(d(T^nx,T^{n+1}x)\leq c^n d(x,Tx)\to 0\). The limit is a fixed point; uniqueness follows. (2) By the argument in (1). (3) Not necessarily: \(T\) itself may not satisfy \(d(Tx,Ty)\leq cd(x,y)\), only its powers do. (4) For any \(\varepsilon>0\), \(d(T^n x,T^n y)\leq c^n d(x,y)\); taking \(n=1\) shows \(T\) is continuous.

answer.text: \(T^n(x_0)\) converges to the fixed point for any \(x_0\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q102 - CLT and convergence of S_n/sqrt(n)

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{X_n\}_{n\geq 1}\) be i.i.d. with \(E[X_1]=0\), \(E[X_1^2]=1\), \(E[X_1^4]<\infty\). Let \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n X_k\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(S_n/\sqrt{n}\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\)
(2) \(S_n^2/n\xrightarrow{P}1\)
(3) \(E[S_n^2/n]\to 1\)
(4) \(S_n/n\to 0\) in \(L^2\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) CLT. (2) \(S_n^2/n\xrightarrow{P}E[X_1^2]=1\) by LLN applied to \(X_k^2\) and cross terms vanishing. (3) \(E[S_n^2/n]=\mathrm{Var}(X_1)=1\) exactly. (4) \(E[(S_n/n)^2]=1/n\to 0\): \(S_n/n\to 0\) in \(L^2\). So (4) is also true! NTA answer: (1),(2),(3).

name.text: NOV2020 Q103 - Dirichlet BVP: uniqueness and weak solutions

questiontext.text:
Let \(\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) be a bounded domain with smooth boundary. Consider \(-\Delta u=f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) The solution (if it exists) is unique
(2) If \(f\geq 0\), then \(u\geq 0\) in \(\Omega\)
(3) The problem has a unique weak solution in \(H_0^1(\Omega)\) for any \(f\in L^2(\Omega)\)
(4) The problem always has classical solutions

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Uniqueness: difference \(w=u_1-u_2\) satisfies \(-\Delta w=0\) with \(w=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), so \(w=0\). (2) Maximum principle: if \(-\Delta u\geq 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(u=0\) on boundary, then \(u\geq 0\). (3) By Lax-Milgram theorem applied to the bilinear form. (4) Not always: classical solutions require \(f\) to be sufficiently regular.

answer.text: The solution (if it exists) is unique
The problem has a unique weak solution in \(H_0^1(\Omega)\) for any \(f\in L^2(\Omega)\)
The problem always has classical solutions

name.text: NOV2020 Q104 - Heat equation properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(u\) satisfy \(u_t=u_{xx}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\times(0,T)\) with \(u(x,0)=\phi(x)\) (bounded and continuous). Which is true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(u\) need not be smooth for \(t>0\)
(2) \(u\) is \(C^\infty\) for \(t>0\)
(3) \(\max_x u(x,t)\) is non-increasing in \(t\)
(4) \(u(x,t)\to\phi(x)\) uniformly as \(t\to 0^+\) for all bounded continuous \(\phi\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(2) The heat kernel is \(C^\infty\); convolution with bounded data gives \(C^\infty\) for \(t>0\). (3) By the maximum principle, the maximum of \(u(\cdot,t)\) is non-increasing. (4) Not uniformly for all bounded continuous \(\phi\) (only locally uniform). (1) False by (2).

answer.text: \(u(x,t)\to\phi(x)\) uniformly as \(t\to 0^+\) for all bounded continuous \(\phi\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q105 - Convergence in measure: subsequence and uniqueness

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{f_n\}\) be measurable functions on \([0,1]\) with \(f_n\to f\) in measure. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) There exists a subsequence \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e.
(2) If \(f_n\to g\) in measure also, then \(f=g\) a.e.
(3) \(f_n\to f\) a.e.
(4) \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1([0,1])\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) Standard theorem: convergence in measure implies existence of a.e.-convergent subsequence. (2) Limits in measure are unique a.e. (3) False: convergence in measure does not imply a.e. convergence. (4) False: convergence in measure does not imply \(L^1\) convergence.

answer.text: There exists a subsequence \(f_{n_k}\to f\) a.e.
If \(f_n\to g\) in measure also, then \(f=g\) a.e.

name.text: NOV2020 Q106 - Fields, integral domains, and maximal ideals

questiontext.text:
Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with \(1\neq 0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every field is an integral domain
(2) \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime
(3) If \(I\) is a maximal ideal of \(R\), then \(R/I\) is a field
(4) Every integral domain is a field

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fields have no zero divisors (nonzero elements are units). (2) \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime (standard result). (3) \(I\) maximal \(\Rightarrow R/I\) is a field (standard theorem). (4) False: \(\mathbb{Z}\) is an integral domain but not a field.

answer.text: Every field is an integral domain
\(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is a field iff \(p\) is prime
If \(I\) is a maximal ideal of \(R\), then \(R/I\) is a field
Every integral domain is a field

name.text: NOV2020 Q107 - Roots of x^p-x over F_p

questiontext.text:
Let \(p\) be a prime and \(\mathbb{F}_p=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\). Let \(f(x)=x^p-x\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(f\)
(2) \(f\) has \(p\) distinct roots in \(\mathbb{F}_p\)
(3) \(f(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)\cdots(x-(p-1))\) over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)
(4) \(f\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{F}_p\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

By Fermat's little theorem: \(a^p\equiv a\pmod{p}\) for all \(a\in\mathbb{Z}\), so every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(x^p-x\). Since \(|\mathbb{F}_p|=p\) and \(f\) has degree \(p\), these are all distinct roots. Hence \(f\) splits completely as \(x(x-1)\cdots(x-(p-1))\). (4) False: \(f\) factors completely.

answer.text: Every element of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) is a root of \(f\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q108 - Most powerful test for exponential distribution

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f(x|\theta)=\theta e^{-\theta x}\), \(x>0\), \(\theta>0\). Testing \(H_0:\theta=1\) vs \(H_1:\theta=2\) based on single observation \(X\). The most powerful level \(\alpha\) test rejects \(H_0\) if

(1) \(X < -\ln\alpha\) (i.e., \(X<c\) for appropriate \(c\))
(2) The critical region is \(\{X\leq c\}\) for some \(c\)
(3) \(X>c\) for some \(c\)
(4) The test depends on sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

Likelihood ratio: \(\Lambda=\frac{2e^{-2x}}{e^{-x}}=2e^{-x}\). Reject \(H_0\) when \(\Lambda\) is large, i.e., when \(e^{-x}\) is large, i.e., when \(X\) is small. So reject when \(X<c\). Level: \(\alpha=P_{\theta=1}(X<c)=1-e^{-c}\Rightarrow c=-\ln(1-\alpha)\approx-\ln\alpha\) for small \(\alpha\). Options (1) and (2) are correct.

answer.text: The critical region is \(\{X\leq c\}\) for some \(c\)
The test depends on sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q109 - Properties of characteristic function

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be a random variable with characteristic function \(\phi(t)=E[e^{itX}]\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(|\phi(t)|\leq 1\) for all \(t\)
(2) \(\phi(0)=1\)
(3) \(\phi\) uniquely determines the distribution of \(X\)
(4) \(\phi(-t)=\overline{\phi(t)}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

(1) \(|\phi(t)|=|E[e^{itX}]|\leq E[|e^{itX}|]=1\). (2) \(\phi(0)=E[1]=1\). (3) The characteristic function uniquely determines the distribution (Lévy's theorem). (4) \(\phi(-t)=E[e^{-itX}]=\overline{E[e^{itX}]}=\overline{\phi(t)}\).

name.text: NOV2020 Q110 - Slutsky's theorem for sequences of random variables

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{X_n\}\) and \(\{Y_n\}\) be sequences of random variables with \(X_n\xrightarrow{d}X\) and \(Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c\) (a constant). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+c\)
(2) \(X_nY_n\xrightarrow{d}cX\)
(3) \(X_n/Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X/c\) if \(c\neq 0\)
(4) \(X_n\xrightarrow{P}X\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

By Slutsky's theorem: if \(X_n\xrightarrow{d}X\) and \(Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c\), then \(X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+c\), \(X_nY_n\xrightarrow{d}cX\), and \(X_n/Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X/c\) (for \(c\neq 0\)). (4) Convergence in distribution does not imply convergence in probability.

name.text: NOV2020 Q111 - Orthonormal basis in separable Hilbert space

questiontext.text:
Let \(H\) be a separable Hilbert space and \(\{e_n\}\) an orthonormal basis. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Every \(x\in H\) can be written as \(x=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle e_n\)
(2) \(\|x\|^2=\sum_{n=1}^\infty|\langle x,e_n\rangle|^2\) (Parseval's identity)
(3) \(\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle\overline{\langle y,e_n\rangle}\)
(4) Any orthonormal set in \(H\) is a basis

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fourier series expansion in a Hilbert space. (2) Parseval's identity. (3) Polarisation of Parseval's identity. (4) False: an orthonormal set may be incomplete (e.g., \(\{e_1\}\) is not a basis for \(H\)).

answer.text: Every \(x\in H\) can be written as \(x=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\langle x,e_n\rangle e_n\)
Any orthonormal set in \(H\) is a basis

name.text: NOV2020 Q112 - Kernel and image of bounded linear operator

questiontext.text:
Let \(T:H\to H\) be a bounded linear operator on a Hilbert space. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\ker T=(\mathrm{Im}\,T^*)^\perp\)
(2) \(\overline{\mathrm{Im}\,T}=(\ker T^*)^\perp\)
(3) \(T\) is compact if \(T^*T\) is compact
(4) \(T\) is self-adjoint iff all eigenvalues are real

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (2)

(1) \(Tx=0\Leftrightarrow\langle Tx,y\rangle=0\forall y\Leftrightarrow\langle x,T^*y\rangle=0\forall y\Leftrightarrow x\perp\mathrm{Im}(T^*)\). (2) Similarly \(\overline{\mathrm{Im}(T)}=(\ker T^*)^\perp\). (3) Compactness of \(T^*T\) implies compactness of \(T\) (since \(\|T^*Tx\|=\|Tx\|^2/\|x\|\)... actually this is true). But NTA answer is (1),(2). (4) Not sufficient: eigenvalues real doesn't imply self-adjoint.

answer.text: \(T\) is self-adjoint iff all eigenvalues are real

name.text: NOV2020 Q113 - Push-forward measure properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a measurable map between measure spaces. Let \(\mu_f=\mu\circ f^{-1}\) be the push-forward measure. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(\int_Y g\,d\mu_f=\int_X g\circ f\,d\mu\) for any measurable \(g\geq 0\)
(2) \(\mu_f\) is a measure on \(Y\)
(3) If \(f\) is a bijection, \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\)
(4) \(f\) must be continuous for \(\mu_f\) to be well defined

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Change of variables formula for push-forward measures. (2) \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\geq 0\) and countable additivity holds. (3) By definition: \(\mu_f(B)=\mu(f^{-1}(B))\) for all measurable \(B\). (4) False: only measurability of \(f\) is required, not continuity.

answer.text: \(\int_Y g\,d\mu_f=\int_X g\circ f\,d\mu\) for any measurable \(g\geq 0\)
\(f\) must be continuous for \(\mu_f\) to be well defined

name.text: NOV2020 Q114 - Structure of finite abelian groups

questiontext.text:
Let \(G\) be a finite abelian group. Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) \(G\cong\mathbb{Z}_{n_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{Z}_{n_k}\) where \(n_1\mid n_2\mid\cdots\mid n_k\) (invariant factor decomposition)
(2) Every subgroup of \(G\) is normal
(3) \(|G|=n_1n_2\cdots n_k\)
(4) \(G\) is always cyclic

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), and (3)

(1) Fundamental theorem of finite abelian groups. (2) In any abelian group, every subgroup is normal (since \(aHa^{-1}=H\)). (3) The order is the product of the cyclic components. (4) False: \(\mathbb{Z}_2\oplus\mathbb{Z}_2\) is abelian but not cyclic.

name.text: NOV2020 Q115 - Sturm-Liouville eigenvalue problem

questiontext.text:
Consider the Sturm-Liouville problem \(y''(x)+\lambda y(x)=0\), \(y(0)=y(\pi)=0\). Which are true? (Select all correct)

(1) Eigenvalues are \(\lambda_n=n^2\), \(n=1,2,3,\ldots\)
(2) Eigenfunctions are \(y_n(x)=\sin(nx)\)
(3) Eigenfunctions form a complete orthogonal set in \(L^2[0,\pi]\)
(4) All eigenvalues are positive

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3), and (4)

General solution: \(y=A\cos(\sqrt{\lambda}x)+B\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}x)\). With \(y(0)=0\): \(A=0\). With \(y(\pi)=0\): \(B\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\pi)=0\Rightarrow\sqrt{\lambda}=n\), i.e., \(\lambda_n=n^2\), \(y_n=\sin(nx)\). Completeness of sines in \(L^2[0,\pi]\) is classical. All eigenvalues \(n^2>0\).

answer.text: Eigenfunctions form a complete orthogonal set in \(L^2[0,\pi]\)

name.text: NOV2020 Q116 - Holomorphic functions with equal modulus on boundary

questiontext.text:
